oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Whoo! Raptor Rehabilitation
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Darden, Jennifer
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 2 (July 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.by Jennifer Darden Pet Bird Association of VirginiaImagine being nestled safely in your bed, when suddenly, your world turns sideways and your home crashes to the ground! That is how this threeday old screech owl began her life and what led her to become Astra, the ambassador owl. Now 6, she was originally admitted with her siblings, who were all raised and released. Astra developed a reoccurring infection in her mouth and could not beprotect them. In most cases, these are birds with injuries that prevent them from surviving in the wild. To keep Education Ambassadors, you must complete a mentorship program with a federally permitted educator. This is a lengthy, in-depth, year -long process that frequently takes longer. Once permitted, you agree to at least 12 presentations a year. There are strict regulations to follow for care, housing and treatment of our Education Ambassadors. Rehabbers are required to submit reports and re-permit regularly. Permits are also required for volunteers who handle and work around the birds.“Shortly after I started rehabbing, I was struck by the fact that a huge percentage of our patients came in due to unintentional human interaction. My hope was to get these birds out to meet individuals who have never really thought much about them and get them thinking about the things that they do in their regular routine that may impact the birds. Meeting these animals up close, allows people to have a better understanding and appreciation for something they may normally never see. Forming a personal bond with an animal brings about inspiration to change.” – Dana Lusher with Nature’s Nanny Wildlife RehabilitationWhile education ambassadors have a bond with their handlers, they are not pets. Their wild instincts are still intact and like all birds of prey, they are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918. One of the country’s oldest environmental laws, the Act makes it illegal to capture or kill any native migratory bird or to possess or destroy its feathers, nest or eggs. Selling, trading or transporting is also prohibited. This important legislation protects over 1000 species of birds, including owls. If you find an injured owl, please call a local wildlife rehabilitator for help.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 2 (2022): Watchbird July 2022; 6-10
